summary: On the morning of September 11, 2001, I was watching TV when I first saw reports about the World Trade Center being hit. At first, I thought it was just a freak accident when the first plane struck, but when the second plane hit, I felt compelled to call Karen to tell her what was happening. The images of the towers being hit were the start of a very intense day for me.

Karen was someone I called early that morning around 9:20 to tell her about the attacks on the World Trade Center. She was at Arlington House and mentioned she had to get off the phone because she saw Joy running, which made me worried. Later, Karen and I took a walk in Arlington National Cemetery to try to process what had happened that day.

Karen was working at Arlington House on the morning of September 11th when I called her to share news about the attacks. It was close to the Pentagon and Arlington National Cemetery, so it was a tense place to be. I later wondered if Arlington House would be closed, but was told all Park Service employees were essential and had to work the next day.
cues: close to Pentagon and Arlington Cemetery

summary: CNN was one of the main news sources I was watching that morning, and they were the first to report that a plane had crashed into the Pentagon. Throughout the day, CNN and other news channels kept updating us with reports about possible bombs near The Mall and the Capitol, which caused a lot of panic. Watching CNN helped me stay informed but also added to the anxiety of the situation.

I saw on CNN that a plane had crashed into the Pentagon, which is very close to Arlington National Cemetery and Arlington House where Karen was. When I drove near the Pentagon the next day, I saw the smoke still coming from the damage, and it was overwhelming—I started crying because the reality of the attack hit me hard. Visitors often ask me about the Pentagon site, but I find it difficult to talk about what happened there.

I work at Arlington National Cemetery, and on September 11th, even though the site was officially open, we had to deal with the shock of the attacks nearby. The next day, the cemetery was closed to visitors, which upset some people who wanted to pay their respects. Karen and I took a walk in the cemetery after the events, and since then, I find it hard to talk to visitors about that day, especially when their curiosity feels morbid.

During the attacks, there were rumors on TV that The Mall was on fire or that there was a bomb there, which made us panic because we live just a ten-minute walk away. Eventually, my roommate calmed everyone down and we looked outside to see there was no smoke or fire at The Mall. It was a relief but also showed how misinformation was spreading that day.

I was very worried about the Capitol Building because my roommate works for Congressional Quarterly and spends a lot of time on Capitol Hill. On the morning of September 11th, there were reports of potential attacks near the Capitol, and I couldn't reach my roommate or a friend who works for a Congressman. It was a tense time not knowing if they were safe.

My roommate is a Defense and International Affairs reporter for Congressional Quarterly and was on Capitol Hill the morning of September 11th. He and other reporters ended up coming to our apartment to set up laptops and report on the unfolding events. It was intense having so many journalists working nonstop in our living room, trying to cover the crisis.

On the morning of September 11th, there were reports on TV that the State Department might be under attack, which added to the fear because it’s a major government building near where I live. The rumors about bombs near the State Department and other locations like The Mall made everyone panic. Thankfully, these turned out to be false alarms, but the uncertainty was very stressful.

I remember September 11, 2001, vividly. I was at home watching TV when the first plane hit the World Trade Center, and by the time the second plane hit, I felt compelled to call Karen. The day was filled with reports of attacks on the Pentagon, The Mall, the Capitol, and the State Department, causing panic and fear. I wasn’t at the sites, but the proximity to these places made the events very personal and emotional for me.

Around mid-afternoon on September 11th, the reporters staying at my apartment and I walked to Safeway to buy groceries and supplies for the long day ahead. We ran into several Congress members there, and the store was crowded with people buying essentials and alcohol. It was a surreal scene, with everyone trying to cope in their own way amid the chaos.

While at Safeway on the afternoon of September 11th, I recognized Sylvia McKenney among the Congress members who were also shopping. It was striking to see politicians and regular people alike just trying to get through the day by buying groceries and supplies. Her presence reminded me of how the whole community was affected by the events.

On September 11th, I learned that all National Park Service employees, including myself, were considered essential and had to report to work the next day despite the attacks. However, when I arrived, the cemetery was closed to visitors, and we mostly did office work. It was a strange and somber day, balancing the need to keep the site running with the shock of what had happened nearby.

The news on September 11th mentioned that all Department of the Interior employees, which includes the National Park Service, were essential and expected to work the following day. This was a bit disappointing because I was hoping for a closure given the circumstances. It showed how government agencies had to continue functioning even during such a crisis.

Living in southwest DC near L'Enfant Plaza, I was very aware of the Metro system during the September 11 attacks because it connects many of the key sites like the Capitol and Pentagon. The Blue Line, in particular, runs close to the Pentagon area where the plane crashed. The Metro was part of the urban landscape that made the events feel very close and real to me.

The Blue Line of the Washington Metro runs near the Pentagon, which was struck on September 11th. Knowing this made the attack feel even closer to home since I live nearby. The presence of the Blue Line highlights how interconnected the city is and how vulnerable some of these transit routes felt during the crisis.

From my apartment in southwest DC, I can look out towards Rosslyn, Virginia, which is near the Pentagon. On September 11th, I saw smoke rising in that general direction after the plane hit the Pentagon. It was a stark reminder of how close the attack was to both sides of the river and how the whole region was affected.

I live in the L'Enfant Plaza area of southwest DC, which is just a short walk from The Mall and the Capitol. On September 11th, being in L'Enfant Plaza meant I was very close to many of the sites involved in the attacks, which made the day feel very intense and frightening. It was from this apartment that my roommate and I watched the news and hosted reporters covering the events.
cues: close to The Mall and Capitol
